branchingScenario.title: PBL: Acute Chest Pain — Clinical Reasoning

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle:
A 55-year-old male presents to the Emergency Department with sudden-onset severe retrosternal chest pain radiating to the left arm, associated with sweating and nausea. Pain started 2 hours ago while climbing stairs. He is a known smoker (30 pack-years) and diabetic.

Work through this clinical scenario to reach the correct diagnosis and management.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle:
You correctly identified STEMI and initiated appropriate emergency management. Key learning points:
- ECG is the first investigation in acute chest pain
- ST elevation in contiguous leads = STEMI
- Primary PCI is the gold standard treatment
- Door-to-balloon time <90 minutes is critical
Your clinical reasoning needs refinement. Review the approach to acute chest pain, focusing on ECG interpretation and STEMI management protocols. Remember: time is myocardium!
This approach could lead to significant delay in treatment. In acute MI, every minute of delay increases myocardial damage. Always prioritise ECG and vital signs assessment first.

params.text:
Initial Assessment

You are the duty medical officer in the Emergency Department. The patient is brought in on a stretcher, looking pale and diaphoretic.

Vitals:

  • BP: 90/60 mmHg
  • HR: 110/min, irregular
  • RR: 24/min
  • SpO₂: 94% on room air
  • Temperature: 37.1°C

The patient is clutching his chest and appears distressed. He rates his pain as 9/10.

branchingQuestion.question: Based on the presentation, what is your first priority investigation?

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: A 12-lead ECG is the single most important initial investigation in acute chest pain. It should be obtained within 10 minutes of arrival. This helps differentiate between STEMI, NSTEMI, and other causes.

feedback.title: Not the best choice
feedback.subtitle: While a chest X-ray is important, it should not delay the ECG. In acute coronary syndromes, the ECG provides immediate diagnostic information and guides urgent treatment decisions.

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: A CTPA is indicated for suspected pulmonary embolism. The clinical presentation here (retrosternal pain radiating to the arm, smoker, diabetic) is more consistent with acute coronary syndrome. Always start with an ECG.

alternatives.text: Send troponin and wait for results

feedback.title: Dangerous delay!
feedback.subtitle: While troponin is important, waiting for results (which take 1-3 hours) before any other action could be fatal. The ECG gives immediate information and should always come first in acute chest pain.

params.text:
ECG Findings

The 12-lead ECG shows:

  • ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF (>2mm)
  • Reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL
  • Heart rate: 108 bpm
  • Sinus tachycardia with occasional PVCs

This pattern is consistent with an inferior STEMI (ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction) involving the right coronary artery territory.

The clock is ticking — time is myocardium!

branchingQuestion.question: You have confirmed inferior STEMI. What is the definitive treatment?

feedback.title: Excellent!
feedback.subtitle: Primary PCI is the gold standard treatment for STEMI when available within 120 minutes. Door-to-balloon time should be <90 minutes. Activate the cath lab immediately!

feedback.title: Acceptable but not optimal
feedback.subtitle: Thrombolysis is used when PCI is not available within 120 minutes. Since we have a cath lab, primary PCI is preferred as it has better outcomes (higher vessel patency, lower re-infarction rates, lower mortality).

feedback.title: Incorrect for STEMI
feedback.subtitle: Conservative management is NEVER appropriate for STEMI. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate reperfusion therapy. Delay in treatment directly increases mortality and infarct size.

feedback.title: Not first-line
feedback.subtitle: Emergency CABG is reserved for specific situations: failed PCI, left main disease, or multivessel disease not amenable to PCI. Primary PCI is the first-line definitive treatment for STEMI.

params.text:
Immediate Management While Preparing for PCI

While the cath lab is being activated, initiate the following MONA protocol:

  • Morphine — 2-4 mg IV for pain relief (use with caution)
  • Oxygen — Only if SpO₂ <94% (our patient qualifies at 94%)
  • Nitroglycerine — Sublingual, but contraindicated here due to hypotension (BP 90/60)
  • Aspirin — 325 mg chewed immediately (antiplatelet)

Also administer:

  • Ticagrelor 180 mg or Clopidogrel 600 mg loading dose
  • Unfractionated heparin bolus
  • IV fluids cautiously (suspect right ventricular involvement in inferior MI)

Key point: In inferior STEMI, always check right-sided leads (V4R) to rule out right ventricular MI. Nitrates and diuretics are contraindicated in RV infarction as they reduce preload.

Chest X-ray Results

The chest X-ray shows:

  • Normal cardiac silhouette
  • Clear lung fields bilaterally
  • No pneumothorax
  • No widened mediastinum (makes aortic dissection less likely)

The chest X-ray is essentially normal, which is expected in acute MI. While this rules out some differentials (pneumothorax, aortic dissection), it does not diagnose the underlying coronary event.

You have lost valuable time. The ECG should have been your first investigation.
